summary: I grew up and attended school in San Francisco, which has always been my home base. The 1906 earthquake and fire left a lasting impression on me about the importance of community resilience here. Later, I served as president of the San Francisco Art Commission and was involved in many cultural initiatives, including supporting the Municipal Band and developing the San Francisco Art Festival.

During my childhood, I spent summers visiting my grandparents in Nevada City, California. Those visits were a cherished part of my early life, providing a contrast to my urban upbringing in San Francisco. It was a place where family connections were strong and I could enjoy a different pace of life.

I attended the University of California, Berkeley for two years after high school. It was an important stepping stone in my education before transferring to the University of Pennsylvania. Berkeley helped me solidify my academic foundation and prepare for business studies.

After Berkeley, I transferred to the University of Pennsylvania, where I completed my education. Later in life, I served as a trustee of the university, helping with alumni relations on the West Coast and supporting building funds and program development. My connection to Penn has been both educational and philanthropic.

entity: Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania
summary: My time at the Wharton School was particularly formative, providing me with a strong foundation in business principles. This education was crucial for my later work with the family business and other corporate boards. Wharton shaped my approach to management and strategy.

I was engaged and married to my wife Doris, who has been a wonderful partner throughout my life. Doris has been a source of great strength and joy, supporting me through my personal and professional endeavors. Our relationship has been central to my life.

Working for the Zellerbach Paper Company was both challenging and rewarding. I started in various roles and eventually helped establish a personnel department to improve operations. Over the years, the company grew significantly, expanding into exports, boxes, paper bags, and paper towels with investments in new machinery and reorganization.

The dock strike of 1971 was a major event that disrupted shipping and logistics, affecting many businesses including ours. It caused delays and financial strain but also underscored the importance of fair labor practices and negotiation. The strike was a significant challenge during my time managing company-union relations.

I served on the California Beaches and Parks Commission, which involved managing bond issue campaigns and working closely with political leaders like Governor Pat Brown. This role deepened my understanding of public service and environmental stewardship in California. It was a meaningful way to contribute to the state's natural resources.

I worked closely with Governor Pat Brown during my time on the California Beaches and Parks Commission. His leadership influenced many public initiatives I was involved with, especially related to bond issues and park management. Collaborating with him was an important part of my political and civic engagement.

I supported political candidates like Jack Javits who aligned with my values and commitment to good governance. Being active in political circles, I aimed to promote civic engagement and effective leadership through backing such figures. Javits represented the kind of public service I believed in.

I was deeply involved with the YMHA, recognizing its vital role in fostering community, culture, and education among Jewish residents. Supporting the YMHA was part of my broader commitment to strengthening Jewish communal life. It was a place that brought people together and enriched our cultural identity.

My involvement with the Jewish Community Center was significant, as it served as a hub for cultural and educational activities. I valued its role in uniting the Jewish community and providing programs that benefited many. Supporting the center was part of my dedication to community development.

Being appointed president of the San Francisco Arts Commission was a great honor. I worked to promote the arts citywide, supporting initiatives like the Municipal Band and music festivals. Despite challenges such as budget constraints and political pressures, we made significant progress in enriching San Francisco's cultural landscape.

As part of my work with the San Francisco Arts Commission, I supported the Municipal Band and proposed music festivals to enhance the city's cultural offerings. The band was an important symbol of community music and tradition. Our efforts aimed to keep such institutions vibrant and accessible.

The Hanks Report was one of the key documents guiding our efforts to develop neighborhood arts and youth programs in San Francisco. It helped us integrate arts into schools and community initiatives, emphasizing the importance of arts education. This report shaped much of our strategic planning in the arts commission.

Alongside the Hanks Report, the McFadyen Report provided valuable recommendations for expanding arts programs in schools and neighborhoods. We used its insights to strengthen youth participation and community arts support. These reports were instrumental in shaping our policies and initiatives.

Ruth Asawa was a remarkable artist and advocate with whom I worked closely to promote art education in San Francisco schools. We shared the belief that exposure to the arts is vital for children's development and creativity. Our collaboration helped advance art programs and community appreciation for the arts.

I helped develop the San Francisco Arts Festival to showcase local talent and bring the community together through art. The festival was an important platform for artists and helped raise public awareness and appreciation. It was part of our broader strategy to enliven the city's cultural scene.

The San Francisco Art Commission Gallery provided a vital space for exhibitions and helped raise the profile of local artists. I supported its development as a venue that could foster artistic expression and community involvement. The gallery was a key element in our efforts to promote the arts citywide.

Recognizing the need for a dedicated performing arts center, we worked on feasibility studies and community polling to build support for the San Francisco Performing Arts Center. We formed a nonprofit to develop facilities including a garage and explored innovative financing methods. The center was envisioned as a hub for music, theater, and dance with excellent acoustics.

The San Francisco Opera Association has been a cultural jewel for the city. During the 1971-72 season, we worked to support the star system and repertoire to attract audiences and maintain high artistic standards. Supporting the Opera was part of our commitment to sustaining San Francisco's vibrant performing arts scene.

Over the years, the Zellerbach Paper Company expanded significantly, moving into exports, boxes, paper bags, and paper towels. I played a role in investing in new machinery and reorganizing systems to improve efficiency and profitability. Managing the company involved balancing operations with labor relations and market demands.

I served on the board of the Newhouse Foundation, helping to shape policies regarding grants and ensuring funds were effectively used to support arts and community projects. This role allowed me to contribute to philanthropic efforts that aligned with my commitment to cultural development. The foundation was an important partner in funding initiatives.

Temple Emanu-El has been an important spiritual and community center in San Francisco. I worked with successive rabbis to build membership and support for the congregation. The temple has played a central role in my religious and community life.

As a trustee of the University of Pennsylvania, I helped with alumni relations on the West Coast and supported building funds and the development of theaters and athletic programs. My ongoing connection to Penn reflects my commitment to education and institutional growth. This role allowed me to give back to the university that shaped my career.

I was active in the San Francisco Better Business Bureau, promoting ethical business practices and supporting the commercial development of the city. This involvement reflected my commitment to integrity and community economic health. The bureau was a key organization in fostering trust between businesses and the public.

My participation in the San Francisco Commercial Club involved supporting the city's commercial growth and networking with business leaders. The club was an important forum for discussing economic issues and promoting San Francisco's business interests. It complemented my other civic and business activities.

I served on the board of Fibreboard during a time when the company was shifting from wood pulp to synthetic fibers. My role involved guiding strategic decisions and managing consolidations in cooperation with other firms. This experience broadened my perspective on industrial innovation and corporate governance.

As a board member of Rayonier, I helped oversee the company's transition from traditional wood pulp products to synthetic fibers. We managed consolidations and worked closely with other firms to strengthen the business. This role was part of my broader involvement in the paper and fiber industries.

I was involved with Joseph Magnin Co. during its expansion and transition to a public company. My responsibilities included managing depreciation, financing expansion, setting meeting schedules, and navigating offers such as the Amfac proposal. These efforts aimed to strengthen the company's market position and financial health.

Amfac made an offer related to Joseph Magnin Co., which I was involved in managing. We carefully considered this proposal as part of our efforts to finance expansion and strengthen the company. The interaction with Amfac was a significant aspect of corporate strategy during that period.
